among-notes-title-n.title: Sweeney Among ...
among-notes-title-n.text: Sweeney Among ... Full title: "Sweeney Among the Nightingales" reference topics full publication history the title Sweeney Discussion of the metre back to "Sweeney Am."

burbank-notes-antony-n.title: The God Hercules...
burbank-notes-antony-n.text: The God Hercules... From William Shakespeare's Antony and Cleopatra: "'Tis the god Hercules, whom Antony lov'd / Now leaves him ." (4.3.30-1). Notice the difference in capitalisation between Shakespeare and Eliot, which is a kind of clarification on Eliot's pa

burbank-notes-aspern-n.title: tra la,
burbank-notes-aspern-n.text: The gondola... cf. end of chapter 1 of The Aspern Papers , Henry James. At this point in the novel, which takes place in Venice, the scholar arrives at the old palace in which the former mistress of his subject, Jeffrey Aspern, still lives with her daughter. E

burbank-notes-burbank-n.title: tra la,
burbank-notes-burbank-n.text: Burbank reference topics discussion of Burbank discussion of Bleistein discussion of Burbank and Bleistein discussion of Baedeker back to "Burbank"

burbank-notes-entertainment-n.title: tra la,
burbank-notes-entertainment-n.text: So the countess... Closing line from John Marston's masque: The Entertainment of Alice, Dowager-Countess of Derby . Niobe is often equated with 'fallen towers', usually Rome, but here a simile with Venice is drawn. The cabinet is the actual poem, which is, lik

burbank-notes-ferdinand-n.title: Sir Ferdinand Klein
burbank-notes-ferdinand-n.text: Sir Ferdinand Klein The most likely candidate for Sir Ferdinand Klein is Francis Ferdinand (1863-1914), heir to the Austrio-Hungarian crown, who was murdered on 28 June 1914 and was known for his diminutative stature ( klein is German for "small"). The histori

burbank-notes-jew-n.title: Jew
burbank-notes-jew-n.text: Jew This line has caused a lot of controversy in the criticism. Bleistein here reminds Burbank of The Merchant of Venice, the speech in which Shylock in turn reminds Antonio of his racist insults. The insults have been paraphrased, like the rest of Bleistein's

burbank-notes-lions-n.title: On the Rialto
burbank-notes-lions-n.text: lion's wings The original draft read lion's mane , and wings was suggested by Pound. The winged lion is the symbol of St. Mark and hence of Venice, of whom St. Mark was the patron saint. The idea is that Venice has been dressed up and domesticised, as for a ca

burbank-notes-othello-n.title: tra la,
burbank-notes-othello-n.text: Goats and ... From Othello , William Shakespeare (4.1.300). These words mark the point at which Iago has convinced Othello that his wife has been unfaithful to him and he decides to return to Venice, where he will eventually kill Desdemona and himself. (Also h

burbank-notes-phoenix-n.title: Defunctive music
burbank-notes-phoenix-n.text: Defunctive music From William Shakespeare's "The Phoenix and the Turtle." The whole stanza reads: " Let the priest in surplice white, / That defunctive music can, / Be the death-divining swan, / Lest the requiem lack his right." reference topics "The Phoenix a

burbank-notes-semite-n.title: Chicago
burbank-notes-semite-n.text: Semite Bleistein is identified as a Semite. Since Jews were always so independent of nation, having none of their own, they have been recognisable throughout much of the history of time and were never afraid to settle in new countries, often without losing the

burbank-notes-sisters-n.title: Tennyson Note
burbank-notes-sisters-n.text: They were... From Alfred Tennyson's "The Sisters". Note that Tennyson's poem reads 'she' instead of 'he.' reference topics Tennyson's The Sisters "The Sisters" and "Burbank" Structural analysis back to "Burbank"

burbank-notes-toccata-n.title: tra la,
burbank-notes-toccata-n.text: with such... From "A Toccata of Galuppi's," Robert Browning. The full line reads "Dear dead women, with such hair too!" reference topics "A Toccata of Galuppi's" "Toccata" and "Burbank" Ruskin's The Stones of Venice Structural analysis back to "Burbank"

burbank-notes-viennese-n.title: Chicago
burbank-notes-viennese-n.text: Viennese Bleistein has a Viennese attribute, which presumably means that he has moved from Vienna to Chicago, or that his immediate ancestor has done so. Vienna was, like Venice and Chicago, an important city of trade and the home of many nationalities and rac

burbank-notes-alltheda-n.title: all the day
burbank-notes-alltheda-n.text: all the day Perhaps an ironic reference to Spenser, Faerie Queene : "All night she spent in bidding of her bedes, / And all the day in doing good and godly deedes." - Volupine has been with Burbank all night and vainly displays her in her shuttered barge all d

burbank-notes-axletree-n.title: axletree
burbank-notes-axletree-n.text: axletree The axletree is the tree of which the axis of Chariots used to be made and in classical times the burning chariot driven by Phoebus was the sun. Eliot refers to a similar image in Seneca's Hercules Furens and Chapman's Bussy D'Ambois , two fragments w

burbank-notes-beatup-n.title: beat up the...
burbank-notes-beatup-n.text: beat up the... The dawn is beat up by the horses of Phoebus, who rides the sun across the sky every day in his chariot, called by Ovid the burning axle-tree. There is another echo from Ovid's Metamorphoses , the story of Niobe: "With even surface, far extendin

burbank-notes-bentknee-n.title: a saggy bending...
burbank-notes-bentknee-n.text: a saggy bending... Symbolises humility. Cf. Dante, Purgatory I.51: " My guide, then laying hold on me, by words / And intimations given with hand and head, / Made my bent knees and eye submissive pay / Due reverence." Saggy colours the representation slightly

burbank-notes-bluenailed-n.title: blue-nailed
burbank-notes-bluenailed-n.text: blue-nailed Cf. Dante, Inferno . "As one, who hath an ague fit so near, / His nails already are turn'd blue, and he / Quivers all o'er, if he but eye the shade" (Canto XVII, 85-7) This is Dante's fear as he climbs the monster Geryon who will bring him and Virg

burbank-notes-bridge-n.title: little bridge...
burbank-notes-bridge-n.text: little bridge... A little bridge in Venice is very common, of course, but a bridge is also a conventional symbol for making a decision, and this one is presumably taken from Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man , where Stephen Daedalus, rather than taking ord

burbank-notes-chicago-n.title: Chicago
burbank-notes-chicago-n.text: Chicago Bleistein is from Chicago, and thus, like Burbank, an American. Chicago at the beginning of the century was a prosperous trade-city and Americans were in fact a minority group - in 1903 the city's population consisted of several racial groups, the larg

burbank-notes-clipped-n.title: clipped
burbank-notes-clipped-n.text: clipped Burbank perceives Venice, symbolised by the winged lion of St. Mark, with clipped wings, a flea'd rump, and pared claws. The idea is that Venice has been dressed up and domesticised, as for a cat-show, an image Eliot may have gotten from Théophile Gaut

burbank-notes-descend-n.title: descending at...
burbank-notes-descend-n.text: descending at... The line presumably refers to Freud's Interpretation of Dreams . In the first draft the line read "And Triton blew his wrinkled shell." against which Pound wrote "if you 'hotel' this rhythm shd be weighted a bit, I think." reference topics Bur

burbank-notes-entertains-n.title: she entertains
burbank-notes-entertains-n.text: she entertains Cf. the line in the epigram from John Marston's "entertainment." Princess Volupine is the Niobe which offers Sir Ferdinand a cabinet, which is the poem of literary Venice we've seen thus far. Ferdinand in response reacts like King Claudius in Ha

burbank-notes-evenfeet-n.title: with even feet
burbank-notes-evenfeet-n.text: with even feet Cf. "Slowly" in the previous stanza. Apart from indicating the steady and inevitable approach of the horses, the line might also be a reference to Emily Dickinson: " We trust, in plumed procession, / For such the angels go, / Rank after rank, wi

burbank-notes-eye-n.title: eye
burbank-notes-eye-n.text: eye Presumably the eye is Bleistein's - however, in the previous stanza Bleistein's features were described in the plural, and 'eyes' would be expected here. Hence it is often thought that this eye does not refer to Bleistein, but someone else. But single eye

burbank-notes-flead-n.title: flea'd
burbank-notes-flead-n.text: flea'd Burbank perceives Venice, symbolised by the winged lion of St. Mark, with clipped wings, a flea'd rump, and pared claws. The idea is that Venice has been dressed up and domesticised, as for a cat-show, an image Eliot may have gotten from Théophile Gauti

burbank-notes-horses-n.title: the horses
burbank-notes-horses-n.text: the horses There are two possible interpretations: the horses may be drawing Phoebus' chariot, but they may also be beating up the dawn on earth, with the sun rising overhead. Hence they would represent the army approaching from Istria, converging Venice's fal

burbank-notes-istria-n.title: Istria
burbank-notes-istria-n.text: Istria Pronounce ['istry-ay] . Peninsula currently belonging to the republic of Croatia, but formerly belonging to Austria-Hungary and Venice. Herod relates in his history how Eatarchus confused the Ister for the Nile and thus Itria for Egypt, whence Antony's

burbank-notes-lights-n.title: Lights, Lights
burbank-notes-lights-n.text: Lights, Lights In Shakespeare's play, Hamlet has had a vision of his father who told him that his uncle killed his father. To find out whether his vision was true, he stages a play for his uncle which contains a similar murder, called the Mousetrap, "a tale of

burbank-notes-lot-n.title: ew...lot
burbank-notes-lot-n.text: J ew...lot ie. the Jew is even lower than the rat - Jews were at times believed to poison people and cause the destruction of cities and nations. Pile also refers to the hair of an animal, for instance the wool of sheep. Again the joke has been made contempora

burbank-notes-loved-n.title: loved him...
burbank-notes-loved-n.text: loved him... The line occurs twice in Shakespeare, in II King Henry IV (3.2.42) and Macbeth (4.3.18). The context of the latter suits the poem slightly better, for it fits the reference to Joyce; in the fromer death is caused by arrows, which may refer to the

burbank-notes-lustreless-n.title: a lustreless...
burbank-notes-lustreless-n.text: a lustreless... Bleistein overviews history with cold and detached reason. Perhaps, as the Marchesa in Poe's Venetian tale "The Assignation," Bleistein sees in the perspective of Canaletto, "the eye, like a shattered mirror, multiplies the images of its sorrow

burbank-notes-money-n.title: Money
burbank-notes-money-n.text: Money in furs "The Jew under the rat" leads to the joke "Money in furs" which Eliot may have picked up close at home - there was a Bleistein fur-trader registered at Lloyd's bank when Eliot worked there. Later, in 1927, another Bleistein moved to London from L

burbank-notes-nilnisi-n.title: Nil nisi Note
burbank-notes-nilnisi-n.text: Nil nisi... Nothing if not the divine is certain; all else [is like] smoke. Inscription on a painting by Andrea Mantegna, the "St Sebastian." reference topics St Sebastian, Andrea Mantegna The Story of St Sebastian Bleistein and St Sebastian back to "Burbank"

burbank-notes-palms-n.title: and elbows, with...
burbank-notes-palms-n.text: and elbows, with... Cf. Sadi, Gulistan : " O thou who showest virtues on the palms of the hand / But concealest thy errors under the armpit / What wilt thou purchase, O vain-glorious fool, / On the day of distress with counterfeit silver?" reference topics Gul

burbank-notes-pared-n.title: tra la,
burbank-notes-pared-n.text: Pared his... Burbank perceives Venice, symbolised by the winged lion of St. Mark, with clipped wings, a flea'd rump, and pared claws. The idea is that Venice has been dressed up and domesticised, as for a cat-show, an image Eliot may have gotten from Théophile

burbank-notes-passed-n.title: passed seaward...
burbank-notes-passed-n.text: passed seaward... presumably from Rostand, Cyrano : " Not Venus' self can mount her conch blown seaward, / As she can step into her chaise a porteurs." The first draft read "passed slowly like the passing bell." reference topics Cyrano and "Burbank" "Burbank's

burbank-notes-passing-n.title: passing bell
burbank-notes-passing-n.text: passing bell Bell rung in moment of person's death. Cf. John Ruskin, The Stones of Venice , who wished to record " the warning which seems to me to be uttered by every one of the fast gaining waves, that beat, Eke passing bells, against the Stones of Venice."

burbank-notes-perspective-n.title: perspective of Canaletto
burbank-notes-perspective-n.text: perspective of Canaletto Giovanni Antonio Canal (1697-1768). Venetian painter whose name is almost synonymous with Venice; cf. Gautier, Le Carnaval de Venise, where he calls Venice: " La ville de Canaletto!" Hence Bleistein has a perspective of the history of

burbank-notes-phthisic-n.title: meagre, phthisic
burbank-notes-phthisic-n.text: meagre, phthisic Burbank's eyes have been opened by Bleistein and finally sees Volupine for who she really is - a declining beauty. Cf. Byron's Childe Harold's Pilgrimage, Canto LXXIX: "The Niobe of Nations! there she stands, / Childless and crownless, in her

burbank-notes-piles-n.title: rats...piles
burbank-notes-piles-n.text: rats...piles The piles of Venice are the heavy beams which support the buildings from sinking into the soft sea-bed of the Adriatic sea. Rats gnawed at these wooden piles, sometimes causing a building to collapse. reference topics back to "Burbank"

burbank-notes-princess-n.title: Princess Volupine
burbank-notes-princess-n.text: Princess Volupine Volupine, from voluptas and "pine," is the personification of Venice, who is traditionally represented in poetry as a woman. Eliot suitably names her "decaying beauty." Princess is, among others, a euphemism for whore. reference topics Prince

burbank-notes-protozoic-n.title: protozoic slime
burbank-notes-protozoic-n.text: protozoic slime PROTOZOIC = "containing the earliest traces of living beings ." Hence the lustreless eye stares from the dawn of living time, in continuance of the evolutionist version of contemporary anti-Semitism. The slime of the Nile was long thought to co

burbank-notes-protrusive-n.title: protrusive
burbank-notes-protrusive-n.text: protrusive If Bleistein is thinking of racism back home in America, he might be thinking of Walt Witman's racistic Salut au Monde (12): " You Austral negro, naked, red, sooty, with protrusive lip, groveling, seeking your food! / You Caffre, Berber, Soudanese!

burbank-notes-rialto-n.title: On the Rialto
burbank-notes-rialto-n.text: On the Rialto Cf. Shakespeare, The Merchant of Venice : "Signoir Antonio, many a time and oft / In the Rialto you have rated me / about my moneys and my usances: / Still I have borne it with a patient shrug" (1.3.101-4) . From his historical perspective of his

burbank-notes-seven-n.title: the seven laws
burbank-notes-seven-n.text: the seven laws In The Stones of Venice , in the chapter on the St. Mark, John Ruskin explains his seven laws of architecture. At the conclusion, comparing Venice to the tall buildings of the North, he wrote: " we have here low walls spread before us like the p

burbank-notes-shuttered-n.title: shuttered barge
burbank-notes-shuttered-n.text: Her shuttered barge... Cf. Enobarbus in Shakespeare, Antony and Cleopatra : "The barge she sat in, like a burnished throne / Burned on the water." (2.2.201-2) . Shuttered, which has an erotic connotation, refers to a gondola, which is the 'barge' used in Venic

burbank-notes-slowly-n.title: Slowly
burbank-notes-slowly-n.text: Slowly Cf. "With even feet," next stanza. Presumably meant to indicate the steady pace of the gondola. The first draft read "Seaward:" reference topics Cyrano and "Burbank" "Burbank's" first draft Princess Volupine discussion of Burbank back to "Burbank"

burbank-notes-smoky-n.title: The smoky candle end of time
burbank-notes-smoky-n.text: the smoky candle end... A complex line, which combines Mantegna's image of history as a burning candle, Shelley's image of esthetic perception as a burning coal, and Bleistein's cigar. It marks that stage at which Bleistein achieves the third of Aquinas aesthe

burbank-notes-thisorsu-n.title: But this or...
burbank-notes-thisorsu-n.text: But this or... Cf. Robert Browning, "How it Strikes a Contemporary": "and this, or something like it, was his way." Bleistein, being misrepresented in the three ways that Joyce describes in the the portrait, himself points out Venice's misrepresentation. This

burbank-notes-times-n.title: Times' ruins
burbank-notes-times-n.text: Times' ruins In The Education of Henry Adams , Adams discusses what time's ruins, of Rome in particular, have taught him: "Young men as a rule saw little in Italy, or anywhere else, and in after life, when Adams began to learn what some men could see, he shran

burbank-notes-title-a.title: Title of “Burbank with a Baedeker: Bleistein with a Cigar”
burbank-notes-title-a.text: Title of "Burbank with a Baedeker: Bleistein with a Cigar" linguistic analysis The syntactic parallelism of the title is striking, and in that particularly the presence of a colon. The syntactic structures of the title's two constituents are exactly the same -

burbank-notes-title-n.title: tra la,
burbank-notes-title-n.text: Burbank with a Baedeker: Bleistein with a Cigar This poem is probably Eliot's most allusionary poem; or, if not the most allusionary, certainly the most selfconscious in the way other texts are evoked - in no other poem meaning has become so dependent on assoc

burbank-notes-trala-n.title: tra la,
burbank-notes-trala-n.text: Tra-la-la... cf. "tra la, tra la, la, la, la laire," from Theophile Gautier's "Variations sur le Carnaval de Venise." reference topics "Carnaval de Venise" "Carnaval de Venise" "Carnaval" and "Burbank" Eliot and the quatrain form back to "Burbank"

burbank-notes-under-n.title: under sea
burbank-notes-under-n.text: under sea From William Shakespeare's Antony and Cleopatra. The music that signifies Hercules' departure (see God Hercules), "i' the air" and "Under the earth" (4.3.14-5), passes "under sea" in Eliot's poem, thus made suitable for Venice. reference topics Anton

waste-notes-belladonna-n.title: Belladonna
waste-notes-belladonna-n.text: Belladonna Jonsonian Italian name meaning "beautiful lady". Belladonna is also an eye cosmetic and a poison - the deadly nightshade. (NA) reference topics The Rock link two back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-ihadnot-n.title: I had
waste-notes-ihadnot-n.text: I had not ... 63. Cf. Inferno III, 55-57: si lunga tratta di gente, ch'io non averei creduto che morte tanta n'avesse disfatta. [Eliot's note] Dante, just outside the gate of hell, has seen "the wretched souls of those who lived without disgrace and without pr

waste-notes-pound-n.title: Pound
waste-notes-pound-n.text: Ezra Pound (1885-1972) Eliot dedicates this poem to Pound not only because he helped give the poem shape, but because Pound has at the time of publication of The Waste Land , 1922, been a tremendous friend and teacher to Eliot for 10 years. They discussed poet

waste-notes-sosostri-n.title: Sosostris
waste-notes-sosostri-n.text: Sosostris A mock Egyptian name (suggested to Eliot by "Sesostris, the Sorceress of Ecbatana," the name assumed by a character in Aldous Huxley's novel Crome Yellow who dresses up as a gypsy to tell fortunes at a fair). (NA) reference topics link one link two b

waste-notes-stanbergersee-n.title: Stanbergersee
waste-notes-stanbergersee-n.text: Starnbergersee Lake a few miles south of Munich, where the "mad" King Ludwig II of Bavaria drowned in 1886 in mysterious circumstances. This romantic, melancholy king was a passionate admirer of Richard Wagner and especially of Wagner's opera Tristan und Isold

waste-notes-sybil-n.title: Nam Sibyl...
waste-notes-sybil-n.text: Nam Sibyl... This opening quote from Petronius' Satyricon (1st century A.D.) sets the scene for the poem - the Sibyl is an old, withered, but immortal woman who is tired with life and wants to die; note the similarites with Gerontion, of which more below. refe

waste-notes-tempest1-2-487-n.title: Those were pearls ...
waste-notes-tempest1-2-487-n.text: Those were pearls ... The line is from Shakespeare's Tempest (I.2.398). Ariel's song to the shipwrecked Ferdinand, who was "sitting on a bank / Weeping again the King my father's wrack," when "this music crept by me on the waters." The song is about the suppos

waste-notes-thereisshad-n.title: Thereisshadow
waste-notes-thereisshad-n.text: There is shadow ... The rock and the importance of its shadow is a theme that reoccurs throughout the whole Bible. In the hot desert lands of the Middle-East a rock that provides shade is very valuable: not only could you find shade from the hot sun, but you c

waste-notes-agameof-n.title: A Game of Chess
waste-notes-agameof-n.text: A Game of Chess The title suggests two plays by Thomas Middleton (1580-1627): A Game at Chess and, more significant, W omen beware Women , which has a scene in which a mother-in-law is distracted by a game of chess while her daughter-in-law is seduced: every m

waste-notes-benefice-n.title: beneficent spider
waste-notes-benefice-n.text: the beneficent spider ... 407. Cf. Webster, The White Devil , V, vi: '...they'll remarry Ere the worm pierce your winding-sheet, ere the spider Make a thin curtain for your epitapths.' [Eliot's note] back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-bradford-n.title: Bradford
waste-notes-bradford-n.text: Bradford Either the Yorkshire woolen manufacturing town, where many fortunes were made in World War I, or the pioneer oil town of Bradford, Pennsylvania, the home of one of Eliot's wealthy Harvard contemporaries, T.E. Hanley. reference topics back to "The Wast

waste-notes-brings-n.title: brings the sailor
waste-notes-brings-n.text: brings the sailor ... This may not appear as exact as Sappho's lines, but I had in mind the 'longshore' or 'dory' fisherman, who returns at nightfall. [Eliot's note] Sappho's poem addressed Hesperus, the evening star, as the star that brings everyone home from

waste-notes-burial-n.title: Burial
waste-notes-burial-n.text: The Burial of the Dead From the Anglican burial service, full title of the service is "The Order for The Burial of the Dead". (NA, Southam) reference topics back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-burning-n.title: Burning burning
waste-notes-burning-n.text: Burning burning burning ... The complete text of the Buddha's Fire Sermon (which corresponds in importance to the Sermon on the Mount) from which these words are taken, will be found translated in the late Henry Clarke Warren's Buddhism in Translation (Harvard

waste-notes-butatmy-n.title: But at my back
waste-notes-butatmy-n.text: But at my back ... Cf. Marvell, To His Coy Mistress. [Eliot's note] Cf. also line 186. reference topics To His Coy Mistress back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-butatmyb-n.title: butatmyback
waste-notes-butatmyb-n.text: But at my back ... An ironic distortion of Andrew Marvell's "To His Coy Mistress", lines 21-22: "But at my back I always hear/ Time's wingèd chariots hurrying near. Cf. also l. 196 (NA) reference topics Andrew Marvell , "To His Coy Mistress" back to "The Waste

waste-notes-bythewaters-n.title: Sjabloon
waste-notes-bythewaters-n.text: By the waters ... Cf. Psalms 137.1, in which the exiled Hebrews mourn for their homeland: "By the rivers of Babylon, there we sat down, yea, we wept, when we remembered Zion." (NA) reference topics back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-cannon-n.title: Cannon Street Hotel
waste-notes-cannon-n.text: Cannon Street Hotel Located near the station that was then chief terminus for travelers to the Continent, hence a favourite meeting place for businesspeople going or coming from abroad. (NA) back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-chair-n.title: The Chair
waste-notes-chair-n.text: The Chair she ... Cf. Antony and Cleopatra , II, ii, l.190. [Eliot's note] "The barge she sat in, like a burnish'd throne,/Burn'd on the water." In the opening lines of this passage Eliot makes a lot of references to Shakespeare's play, particularly the follow

waste-notes-cif-n.title: C.i.f.
waste-notes-cif-n.text: C.i.f. ... The currants were quoted at a price 'cost insurance and freight to London'; and the Bill of Lading, etc., were to be handed to the buyer upon payment of the sight draft. [Eliot's note] back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-cocorico-n.title: Co co ri co
waste-notes-cocorico-n.text: Co co ri co ... The crowing of the cock signals the departure of ghosts and evil spirits. Cf. Hamlet 1.1.157ff. back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-coirolan-n.title: Coirolanus
waste-notes-coirolan-n.text: Coriolanus Coriolanus, who acted out of pride rather than duty, is an obvious example of a man locked in the prison of his own self. He led the enemy against his native city out of injured pride (cf. Shakespeare, Coriolanus ). (NA) back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-datta-n.title: Datta
waste-notes-datta-n.text: Datta, dayadhvam, damyata ... 'Datta, dayadhvam, damyata' (Give, sympathise, control). The fable of the meaning of the Thunder is found in the Brihadaranyaka - Upanishad , 5, I. A translation is found in Deussen's S echzig Upanishads des Veda, p. 489 [Eliot's

waste-notes-deadsoun-n.title: dead sound
waste-notes-deadsoun-n.text: dead sound A phenomenon which I have often noticed. [Eliot's note] reference topics link two back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-deadtree-n.title: Dead tree
waste-notes-deadtree-n.text: The dead tree ... 23. Cf. Ecclesiastes XII, v. [Eliot's note] The line in the Bible that Eliot refers to contains the priest's warning against the desolation of old age ( NA ). reference topics Ecclesiastes XII, i-x link two back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-deathby-n.title: Death by Water
waste-notes-deathby-n.text: Death by Water ... This section has been interpreted in two ways: either it signifies death by water without resurrection (water misused) or it symbolises the sacrificial death that precedes rebirth. It is true that Phlebas is purged of his commercial interest

waste-notes-elizabeth-n.title: Elizabeth and Leicester
waste-notes-elizabeth-n.text: Elizabeth and Leicester V. Froude, Elizabeth , Vol. I, ch. iv, letter of De Quadra to Philip of Spain: "In the afternoon we were in a barge, watching the games on the river (The Queen) was alone with Lord Robert and myself on the poop, when they began to talk

waste-notes-emptychapel-n.title: empty chapel
waste-notes-emptychapel-n.text: there is the empty ... Suggesting the moment of near despair before the Chapel Perilous, when the questing knight sees nothing there but decay. This illusion of nothingness is the knight's final test. back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-etoces-n.title: Et O ces
waste-notes-etoces-n.text: Et O ces ... V. Verlaine, Parsifal . [Eliot's note] Verlaine's sonnet describes Parsifal, the questing knight, resisting all sensual temptations to keep himself pure for the Grail; Wagner's Parsifal had his feet washed before entering the castle of the Grail.

waste-notes-fabbro-n.title: Il miglior fabbro
waste-notes-fabbro-n.text: Il miglior fabbro Italian for "the better craftsman," which Pound certainly was. There were few poets in Eliot's day with such expert knowledge of the craft as Ezra Pound, who studied in depth the manifestations of poetry in different and often exotic language

waste-notes-fireserm-n.title: firesermon
waste-notes-fireserm-n.text: The Fire Sermon The Fire Sermon was preached by the Buddha against the fires of lust and other passions that destroy people and prevent their regeneration. reference topics Kassapa The Fire Worshipper Matthew 5-7, The Sermon on the Mount back to "The Waste Lan

waste-notes-fishing-n.title: Fishing with the
waste-notes-fishing-n.text: Fishing, with the ... V. Weston: From Ritual to Romance ; chapter on the Fisher King. [Eliot's note] back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-flung-n.title: Flung their smoke
waste-notes-flung-n.text: Flung their smoke ... Laquearia. V. Aeneid , I, 726: dependent lychni laqueribus auries incensi, et noctem flammis funalia vincunt [Eliot's note] Eliot refers to lines from a passage which describe how Dido entertains her royal guests (Aeneas and train). Eliot

waste-notes-gammon-n.title: Gammon
waste-notes-gammon-n.text: Gammon Ham or bacon. (NA) reference topics back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-goodnigh-n.title: Good night
waste-notes-goodnigh-n.text: Good night... Cf. the mad Ophelia's departing words in Shakespeare's Hamlet : OPHELIA I hope all will be well. We must be patient; but I cannot choose but weep to think they would lay him i' th' cold ground. My brother shall know of it; and so I thank you for

waste-notes-hanged-n.title: Hanged
waste-notes-hanged-n.text: The Hanged Man On his card in the Tarot pack he is shown hanging from one foot from a T-shaped cross. He symbolizes the self-sacrifice of the fertility god who is killed in order that his resurrection may bring fertility once again to land and people. (NA) ref

waste-notes-hewhowas-n.title: He who was
waste-notes-hewhowas-n.text: He who was ... These lines, containing allusions to Christ's imprisonment and trial, and to Gethsemane and Golgotha, suggest the hopeless days between Good Friday and Easter, between the Crucifixion and the Resurrection - associated with the death of the Fishe

waste-notes-highbury-n.title: Highbury bore me
waste-notes-highbury-n.text: Highbury bore me ... Cf. Purgatorio , V. 133: "Ricorditi di me, che son la Pia; Siena mi fe', disfecemi Maremma." [Eliot's note] Highbury : aresidential London suburb; Richmond : a pleasant part of London westward up the Thames, with boating and riverside hote

waste-notes-hofgarten-n.title: Hofgarten
waste-notes-hofgarten-n.text: Hofgarten A small public park in Munich. (NA) reference topics back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-hurryup-n.title: Hurry up
waste-notes-hurryup-n.text: Hurry up please... The traditional call of the British bartender at closing time. (NA) reference topics The Hollow Men back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-ichbin-n.title: Ichbin
waste-notes-ichbin-n.text: German "I am not Russian at all; I come from Lithuania, a true German." (NA) reference topics back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-jugjug-n.title: Jug Jug
waste-notes-jugjug-n.text: "Jug Jug" Conventional representation of nightingale's song in Elizabethan poetry. (NA) The 'dirty ears are probably Tereus', who raped Philomel. reference topics link two back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-keepthedog-n.title: Keep the dog
waste-notes-keepthedog-n.text: Keep the dog ... Cf. the Dirge in Webster's White Devil. [Eliot's note] In the play by John Webster (d. 1625), the dirge, sung by Cornelia, has the lines "But keep the wolf far hence, that's foe to men, /For with his nails he'll dig them up again." Eliot makes

waste-notes-ladyofro-n.title: Ladyofrocks
waste-notes-ladyofro-n.text: Lady of the Rocks The title "Lady of the Rocks" suggests Madonna (the Virgin Mary) and, therefore, the Madonna of the Rocks, as in Leonardo da Vinci's painting; the rocks symbolize the church (see note above). reference topics The Rock link two back to "The Wa

waste-notes-latourab-n.title: latourabolie
waste-notes-latourab-n.text: a la tour abolie ... V. Gerard de Nerval, Sonnet El Desdichado. [Eliot's note] One of the cards in the Tarot pack is "the tower struck by lightning." (NA) back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-londonbridge-n.title: London bridge is
waste-notes-londonbridge-n.text: London bridge is ... One of the later lines of this nursery rhyme is "Take the key and lock her up, my fair lady." back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-magnus-n.title: Magnus Martyr
waste-notes-magnus-n.text: Of Magnus Martyr ... The interior of St. Magnus Martyr is to my mind one of the finest among Wren's interiors. See The Proposed Demolition of Nineteen City Churches : (P.S. King & Son, Ltd.). [Eliot's note] In these lines, the "pleasant" music, the "fishmen" r

waste-notes-margate-n.title: Margate
waste-notes-margate-n.text: Margate Popular seaside resort on Thames estuary. Eliot and his wife spent a month here in 1921 (see Peter Ackroyd, T.S. Eliot , page 114-5) reference topics back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-mountains-n.title: In the Mountains
waste-notes-mountains-n.text: In the mountains ... A translation of the opening of a Bavarian folksong celebrating King Ludwig and lamenting his drowning. reference topics back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-mylae-n.title: Mylae
waste-notes-mylae-n.text: Mylae The battle of Mylae (260 B.C.) in the First Punic War, which, like World War I, was fought for economic reasons. (NA) reference topics link two back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-olordthou-n.title: O lord thou pluckest
waste-notes-olordthou-n.text: O lord thou pluckest ... From St. Augustine's Confessions again. The collocation of these two representatives of eastern and western asceticism, as the culmination of this part of the poem, is not an accident. [Eliot's note] back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-oneeye-n.title: Oneeye
waste-notes-oneeye-n.text: One-eyed merchant I.e., Mr. Eugenides, "one-eyed" because the figure is in profile on the card and also a suggestion of evil or crookedness, or a pirate. (NA) reference topics link two back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-ontheking-n.title: on the king
waste-notes-ontheking-n.text: And on the King ... Cf. The Tempest , I, ii. [Eliot's note] This is one of many references to Shakespeare's play. Again it is implied that Ferdinand is speaking these lines, and here refers to his relatives that he thinks have drowned. reference topics Shakesp

waste-notes-packof-n.title: Pack of cards
waste-notes-packof-n.text: Pack of cards The four suits of the Tarot pack, discussed by Jessie Weston in From Ritual to Romance, are the cup, lance, sword, and dish - the life symbols found in the Grail story. Weston noted that "today the Tarot has fallen somewhat into disrepute, being

waste-notes-pearls-n.title: Pearls
waste-notes-pearls-n.text: Those are pearls ... Cf. Part I, l. 37,48. [Eliot's note] These lines are from The Tempest again. reference topics The Tempest and The Waste Land back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-philomel-n.title: Philomel
waste-notes-philomel-n.text: Philomel V. Ovid, Metamorphoses , VI, Philomela. [Eliot's note] Eliot refers to the story of Philomela who was raped by Tereus, the husband of her sister Procne. All three are in the end turned into birds; Procne and Philomel into nightingales, and Tereus into

waste-notes-phoenici-n.title: Phoenician
waste-notes-phoenici-n.text: P hoenician Sailor See part 4. Phlebas the Phoenician and Mr. Eugenides, the Smyrna merchant - both of whom appear later in the poem - are different phases of the same symbolic character, here identified as the "Phoenician Sailor." Mr. Eugenides exports "curra

waste-notes-poisascose-n.title: Poi s'ascose nel ...
waste-notes-poisascose-n.text: Poi s'ascose nel ... V. Purgatorio , XXVI, 148. "Ara vos prec per aquella valor que vos guida al som de l'esalina, sovenha vos a temps de ma dolor. Poi s'ascose nel foco che li affina." [Eliot's note] The purgatorial vision of refining fire - as distinct from

waste-notes-quandofi-n.title: Quandofiam
waste-notes-quandofi-n.text: Quando fiam ... V. Pervigilium Veneris . Cf. Philomela in Parts II and III. [Eliot's note] Pervigilium Veneris (Vigil of Venus) is an anonymous late Latin poem combining a hymn to Venus with a description of spring. In the last two stanzas of the Pervigilium o

waste-notes-ratsalle-n.title: rat's alley
waste-notes-ratsalle-n.text: rat's alley Cf. Part III, l. 204 [Eliot's note] The reference in this note and the following also recall the following lines from The Hollow Men : "As wind in dry grass/ Or rats' feet over broken glass/ In our dry cellar" reference topics The Hollow Men back t

waste-notes-riversweats-n.title: The river sweats
waste-notes-riversweats-n.text: The river sweats ... The Song of the (three) Thames-daughters begins here. From line 292 to 306 inclusive they speak in turn. V. Götterdämmerung , III, i: the Rhine Daughters. [Eliot's note] Eliot parallel's the Thames-daughters with the Rhine-maidens in Wagne

waste-notes-rudely-n.title: Rudely
waste-notes-rudely-n.text: So rudely forced Cf. Part III, l. 195. [Eliot's note] The reference in this note and the previous also recall the following lines from The Hollow Men : "As wind in dry grass/ Or rats' feet over broken glass/ In our dry cellar" reference topics Ovid's Metamorph

waste-notes-saintmar-n.title: SaintMary
waste-notes-saintmar-n.text: Saint Mary Woolnoth St. Mary Woolnoth is a church in the "Unreal City" of London (the financial district); the crowd is flowing across the London Bridge to work in the City. (NA) reference topics link two back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-shakespehe-n.title: Shakespeherian
waste-notes-shakespehe-n.text: Shakespeherian Rag American ragtime song, which was a hit of Ziegfeld's Follies in 1912. (NA) reference topics The Hollow Men back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-shalliat-n.title: Shall I at least
waste-notes-shalliat-n.text: Shall I at least ... Cf. Isaiah 38.1: "Thus saith the Lord, Set thine house in order; for thou shalt die, and not live." The inclusive "I," who sits in the symbolic act of fishing (seeking salvation, regeneration, eternity) with the Waste Land behind him, wond

waste-notes-shantih-n.title: Shantih Shantih
waste-notes-shantih-n.text: Shantih shantih ... Shantih. Repeated as here, a formal ending to an Upanishad. 'The Peace which passeth understanding' is our equivalent to this word. [Eliot's note] The Upanishads are poetic dialogues on Hindu scriptures, written after the Vedas, the ancient

waste-notes-sighs-n.title: Sighs
waste-notes-sighs-n.text: Sighs, short and ... Cf. Inferno IV, 25-27: Quivi, secondo che per ascolatre, non avea piante mai che di sospiri che l'aura eterna facevan tremare. [Eliot's note] In Limbo, the first circle of hell, Dante has found the virtuous heathens, who lived before Chris

waste-notes-smyrna-n.title: Smyrna
waste-notes-smyrna-n.text: Smyrna. Seaport in western Turkey; here associated with Carthage and the ancient Phoenician and Syrian merchants (unlike those of modern Smyrna), who spread the old mystery cults. The sort of cult spread by Mr. Eugenides is indicated by his suggestion of "a we

waste-notes-sonofman-n.title: Son of Man
waste-notes-sonofman-n.text: Son of Man Cf. Ezekiel II, i. [Eliot's note] reference topics Son of Man Ezekiel II, i-x back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-soundof-n.title: the sound of
waste-notes-soundof-n.text: The sound of ... Cf. Day, Parliament of Bees: 'When of the sudden, listening, you shall hear, 'A noise of horns and hunting, which shall bring 'Acteaon to Diana in the spring, 'Where all shall see her naked skin...' [Eliot's note] In the Greek myth, Actaeon wa

waste-notes-summer-n.title: Summer surprised us
waste-notes-summer-n.text: Summer surprised us ... Eliot had met King Ludwig's second cousin Countess Marie Larisch and talked with her. Although he had probably not read the countess's book My Past, which discusses King Ludwig at length, he got information about her life and times from

waste-notes-swallow-n.title: swallow
waste-notes-swallow-n.text: swallow swallow ... Cf. Swinburne's "Itylus", which begins, "Swallow, my sister, O sister swallow, / How can thine heart be full of spring?" and Tennyson's lyric in The Princess "O Swallow, Swallow, flying, flying south." back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-sweetthames-n.title: Sweet Thames
waste-notes-sweetthames-n.text: Sweet Thames ... V. Spenser, Prothalamion . [Eliot's note] Eliot's line is the refrain from Spenser's marriage song, which is also set by the Thames in London - but a very different Thames from the modern littered river (NA). reference topics Spenser, Prothala

waste-notes-sylvan-n.title: Sylvan Scene
waste-notes-sylvan-n.text: Sylvan Scene Sylvan scene. V. Milton, Paradise Lost, IV, 140. [Eliot's note] In this section of Paradise Lost we get a description of Eden throught the eyes of the Devil. reference topics Paradise Lost, IV 130-70 back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-tempest-2-1-n.title: The Tempest
waste-notes-tempest-2-1-n.text: William Shakespeare, The Tempest SCENE 2: The Island. Before PROSPERO'S cell Enter PROSPERO and MIRANDA MIRANDA. If by your art, my dearest father, you have Put the wild waters in this roar, allay them. The sky, it seems, would pour down stinking pitch, But th

waste-notes-tereu-n.title: Tereu
waste-notes-tereu-n.text: Tereu ... Latin vocative from of Tereus, who "rudely forc'd" Philomela; it was also one of the conventional words for a nightingale's song in Elizabethan poetry. Cf. the song from John Lyly's Alexander and Campaspe (1564): Oh, 'tis the ravished nightingale. Ju

waste-notes-thatcorp-n.title: thatcorpse
waste-notes-thatcorp-n.text: That corpse ... A distortion of the ritual death of the fertility god. (NA) reference topics link two back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-thekey-n.title: I have heard
waste-notes-thekey-n.text: I have heard ... Cf. Inferno, XXXIII, 46: "ed io senti chiavar l'uscio di sotto / all'orribile torre." Also F.H. Bradley, Appearance and Reality, p. 306: "My external sensations are no less private to my self than are my thoughts or my feelings. In either case

waste-notes-thesefragments-n.title: these fragments
waste-notes-thesefragments-n.text: these fragments ... This may refer to the whole poem - fragments assembled by the speaker in the attempt to come to terms with his situation. (NA) back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-theywash-n.title: They wash their
waste-notes-theywash-n.text: They wash their ... I do not know the origin of the ballad from which these lines are taken: it was reported to me from Sydney, Australia. [Eliot's note] One of the less bawdy versions of the song, which was popular among Australian troops in World War I, went

waste-notes-thismusic-n.title: This music crept
waste-notes-thismusic-n.text: This music crept ... V. The Tempest , as above. [Eliot's note] Cf. line 48. The line is from Ferdinand's speech, continuing after "weeping again the King my father's wrack." Shakespeare, The Tempest back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-threestaves-n.title: three staves
waste-notes-threestaves-n.text: The man with three staves Life-force symbol, associated by Eliot with the Fisher King. (NA) reference topics link two back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-tiresias-n.title: Tiresias
waste-notes-tiresias-n.text: Tiresias Tiresias is a man from Greek mythology who for seven years changed sex, after he struck two intertwined snakes. He is known for being a seer and occurs in many classical texts. In Ovid's Metamorphoses he is asked whether men or women experience more p

waste-notes-title-n.title: tra la,
waste-notes-title-n.text: The Waste Land According to Eliot, the title was suggested by Miss Jessie L. Weston's From Ritual to Romance , a work of literary criticism exploring the pagan and Christian roots of the Grail legend. See also Eliot's own note, below. reference topics full pub

waste-notes-tocarthage-n.title: To Carthage then
waste-notes-tocarthage-n.text: To Carthage then ... V. St. Augustine's Confessions : 'to Carthage then I came, where a cauldron of unholy loves sang all about mine ears.' [Eliot's note] The passage from the Confessions quoted here occurs in St. Augustine's account of his youthful life of lu

waste-notes-unreal-n.title: Unreal City
waste-notes-unreal-n.text: Unreal City Cf. Baudelaire: 'Fourmillante cité, cité pleine de rêves,' 'Où le spectre en plein jour raccroche le passant.' [Eliot's note]. The lines Eliot quotes are from "Les Sept Vieillards" ("The Seven Old Men") by Charles Baudelaire (1821-67); it is poem 9

waste-notes-weshallplay-n.title: Game of Chess
waste-notes-weshallplay-n.text: a game of chess Cf. the game of chess in Middleton's Women beware Women . [Eliot's note] The piece has a scene in which a mother-in-law is distracted by a game of chess while her daughter-in-law is seduced: every move in the chess game represents a move in the

waste-notes-whatisthatsound-n.title: what is that sound
waste-notes-whatisthatsound-n.text: what is that sound ... Cf. Herman Hesse, Blick ins Chaos : 'Schon ist halb Europa, schon ist zumindest der halbe Osten Europas auf dem Wege zum Chaos, fährt betrunken im heiligen Wahn am Abgrund entlang und singt dazu, singt betrunken und hymnisch wie Dmitri K

waste-notes-whatthethunder-n.title: What the Thunder said
waste-notes-whatthethunder-n.text: What the Thunder said ... In the first part of Part V three themes are employed: the journey to Emmaus, the approach to the Chapel Perilous (see Miss Weston's book) and the present decay of eastern Europe. [Eliot's note] The journey to Emmaus (see line 360 and

waste-notes-wheel-n.title: Wheel
waste-notes-wheel-n.text: The Wheel The wheel of fortune, whose turning represents the reversals of human life. (NA) reference topics link two back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-whenlovely-n.title: When lovely woman
waste-notes-whenlovely-n.text: When lovely woman ... V. Goldsmith, the song in The Vicar of Wakefield. [Eliot's note] Olivia, a character in Oliver Goldsmith's novel, sings the following song when she returns to the place where she was seduced: When lovely woman stoops to folly And finds to

waste-notes-whenthehermit-n.title: when the hermit
waste-notes-whenthehermit-n.text: when the hermit-thrush ... This is Turdus aonalaschkae pallasii , the hermit-thrush which I have heard in Quebec Province. Chapman says ( Handbook of Birds of Eastern North America) 'it is most at home in secluded woodland and thickety retreats.... Its notes a

waste-notes-whoisthethird-n.title: Who is the third
waste-notes-whoisthethird-n.text: Who is the third ... The following lines were stimulated by the account of one of the Antarctic expeditions (I forget which, but I think one of Shackleton's): it was related that the party of explorers, at the extremity of their strength, had the constant delu

waste-notes-whythen-n.title: Why then
waste-notes-whythen-n.text: Why then ... V. Kyd's Spanish Tragedy. [Eliot's note] Subtitled "Hieronymo's Mad Againe," Kyd's play (1594) is an early example of the Elizabethan tragedy of revenge. Hieronymo, driven mad by the murder of his son, has his revenge when he is asked to write a c

waste-notes-windunde-n.title: Wind under
waste-notes-windunde-n.text: The wind under ... Cf. Webster: 'Is the wind in that door still?' [Eliot's note] The line cited in the note is from John Webster, The Devil's Law Case 3.2.162. (NA) reference topics The Hollow Men back to "The Waste Land"

waste-notes-youhypocrite-n.title: youhypocrite
waste-notes-youhypocrite-n.text: You! Hypocrite ... V. Baudelaire, Preface to Fleurs du Mal . [Eliot's note] The passage is the last line of the introductory poem "Au Lecteur" ("To the Reader") in Baudelaire's Fleurs du Mal . "Au Lecteur" describes man as sunk in stupidity, sin, and evil, but
